text: This is a case of a sixty-three-year-old male who admitted to the ER with the complaint of left lower quadrant pain. He had a history of three episodes of diverticulitis and no history of surgery. The abdomen was tender and there was defense in the left lower quadrant as well as rebound tenderness. In blood tests, high white blood cells count and increased CRP were detected.

This is a case of a sixty-three-year-old male who admitted to the ER with the complaint of left lower quadrant pain. He had a history of three episodes of diverticulitis and no history of surgery. The abdomen was tender and there was defense in the left lower quadrant as well as rebound tenderness. In blood tests, high white blood cells count and increased CRP were detected.

Abdominal CT revealed pelvic abscess and suspected perforation due to diverticulitis. This CT scan shows the diverticulitis of descending and sigmoid columns and also pelvic abscess secondary to the perforation.

Abdominal CT revealed pelvic abscess and suspected perforation due to diverticulitis. This CT scan shows the diverticulitis of descending and sigmoid columns and also pelvic abscess secondary to the perforation.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection of dense intra-abdominal adhesions, focusing on the mesocolon, which is being carefully separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins the procedure by performing sharp dissection of dense intra-abdominal adhesions, focusing on the mesocolon.
Pus visibly flows from the pelvic abscess, which is then aspirated using a surgical instrument.
Pus visibly flows out from the pelvic abscess, followed by its aspiration using a surgical instrument.
The surgeon dissects the left colic and sigmoidal arteries, applying purple hemoclips to ligate them due to their large diameters. The vessels are then divided using an energy device.
The surgeon dissects the left colic and sigmoidal arteries, applying purple hemoclips to each due to their large diameters. The vessels are then divided using an energy device.
The surgeon carefully divides Toldt's fascia, revealing a cleared pelvic area following the drainage of the abscess.
The surgeon carefully divides Told's fascia, revealing a cleared pelvic area following the drainage of the abscess.
The surgeon prepares the rectosigmoid colon for resection, indicating the area is being isolated and mobilized in preparation for removal.
The surgeon prepares the rectosigmoid colon for resection, indicating the area is being isolated and mobilized for the upcoming surgical step.

The surgeon completes the distal resection using a GIA stapler, creating a six-centimeter staple line. Concurrently, a pharyngeal incision is made, and the resected specimen is extracted from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon completes the distal resection using a GIA stapler, creating a six-centimeter segment. Concurrently, a pharyngeal incision is made, and the resected specimen is extracted from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon determines the proximal border for resection at the middle of the descending colon. An envelope circular stapler is placed at the proximal end of the colon, and the colon is reintroduced into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon determines the proximal border for resection at the middle of the descending colon. An envelope circular stapler is placed at the proximal end of the colon, and the colon is reintroduced into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs an end-to-end brictocolic anastomosis using a 31mm circular stapler. An air leak test is conducted to visually assess the integrity of the anastomosis.
